source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
title: Ensure Network Security is Enabled

input_row.source: CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
input_row.title: Ensure Network Security is Enabled
input_row.description: This helps ensure that all the necessary security measurements are taken to prevent a cyber-attack. Such as utilizing VPC, creating certain inbound and outbound rules, and ACLs.
input_row.rationale: Impact: Provides privacy and lets the user customize their security preferences. Prevents private network from interfering with public networks.
input_row.audit: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console • Sign in to the AWS Management Console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/ with your AWS account credentials. 2. Open the Amazon Neptune Console • Navigate to the service using the Find Services search bar or by directly accessing the console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/neptune/. 3. Select the Neptune Cluster • Choose the Amazon Neptune cluster for which you want to configure network security. • Click on the cluster name to access its details page. 4. Configure Security Groups • In the cluster details page, navigate to the Connectivity & Security or Network & Security section. • Under Security Groups, click on Manage security groups. • Click on Create new security group or select an existing security group associated with your Neptune cluster. • Configure inbound and outbound rules within the security group to control network traffic. o For inbound rules, specify the allowed source IP addresses or security groups and the necessary ports for accessing the Neptune cluster.  o For outbound rules, define the allowed destination IP addresses or security groups and the required ports for outbound connections from the Neptune cluster. • Save the security group settings. 5. Configure Network Access Control Lists (ACLs) • In the cluster details page, navigate to the Connectivity & Security or Network & Security section. • Under Network Access Control Lists (ACLs), click on Manage network ACLs. • Create a new network ACL or select an existing one associated with your Amazon Neptune cluster. • Configure inbound and outbound rules within the network ACL to control network traffic at the subnet level. • Define rules based on IP address ranges, protocols, and ports to allow or deny specific traffic. • Consider security best practices and compliance requirements when configuring the network ACL rules. • Save the network ACL settings. 6. Verify Network Security Configuration • Review the security group and network ACL settings to ensure they align with your security requirements. • Confirm that the inbound and outbound rules only allow necessary traffic and deny unauthorized access. • Verify that your Neptune cluster's security groups and network ACLs are correctly configured. 7. Test Network Connectivity • Launch an Amazon EC2 instance within the same VPC and subnet as your Neptune cluster, or use an existing one. • Connect to the EC2 instance using SSH or other remote access methods. • Test the network connectivity to your Neptune cluster by attempting to connect to it using the appropriate client or utility. • Ensure that the network security settings allow the necessary traffic and deny unauthorized access.
input_row.impact: Provides privacy and lets the user customize their security preferences. Prevents private network from interfering with public networks.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be verified using AWS APIs, as it involves checking the configurations of network-related resources such as security groups and network access control lists (ACLs) associated with Amazon Neptune clusters. These configurations are standard AWS service properties that can be queried and checked programmatically using the boto3 SDK or AWS CLI.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_neptune_cluster_network_security_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS API Calls/Methods to Use:**
  - Use `boto3.client('neptune')` to list Neptune clusters with `describe_db_clusters()`.
  - For each cluster, inspect the associated VPC and security groups using `boto3.client('ec2')`:
    - Use `describe_security_groups()` to retrieve inbound and outbound rules for each associated security group.
    - Use `describe_network_acls()` to retrieve network ACL rules for the relevant subnets.
  
- **AWS CLI Commands:**
  - `aws neptune describe-db-clusters` to list clusters and retrieve VPC and subnet information.
  - `aws ec2 describe-security-groups` to verify security group rules.
  - `aws ec2 describe-network-acls` to verify network ACL rules.

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
  - All Neptune clusters across the account.
  - Security groups and network ACLs assigned to the subnets where Neptune clusters are deployed.

- **Exact Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
  - Ensure security groups have restrictive inbound and outbound rules that allow only necessary traffic and deny unauthorized access.
  - Ensure network ACLs align with security best practices, denying unwanted traffic at the subnet level.

- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - **Pass:** Security groups and network ACLs only allow necessary traffic as per security requirements.
  - **Fail:** Security groups or network ACLs are overly permissive, allowing broad access or unauthorized traffic. 

By automating these checks, one can ensure consistent and comprehensive assessment of network security configurations for Neptune clusters, quickly identifying any configurations that deviate from defined security standards.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The configurations of network-related resources such as security groups and network ACLs can indeed be checked programmatically using AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI. These are standard AWS service properties that can be queried and verified without manual intervention.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8
- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of `boto3.client('neptune')` with `describe_db_clusters()` is appropriate for listing Neptune clusters. Similarly, using `boto3.client('ec2')` with `describe_security_groups()` and `describe_network_acls()` is valid for retrieving security group and network ACL configurations.
- The AWS CLI commands listed (`aws neptune describe-db-clusters`, `aws ec2 describe-security-groups`, `aws ec2 describe-network-acls`) are accurate for the intended checks.
- The program name `aws_neptune_cluster_network_security_configured` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific to the exact security checks being performed.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- The program name could be improved for clarity and specificity. A suggestion would be `aws_neptune_network_security_restrictions` to better reflect the focus on restrictive security configurations.
- Consider checking for the presence of AWS Config rules that might already be monitoring these configurations, which can enhance the automation process.
- Ensure that the checks for security groups and network ACLs include verification against specific security policies or compliance standards that define "necessary traffic."
- It might be beneficial to include checks for VPC flow logs to ensure that logging is enabled for monitoring traffic patterns and identifying potential security issues.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- Given the technical accuracy and the appropriate use of AWS APIs and CLI commands, I have high confidence in the assessment's approach and execution.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Modify: Update the program name to `aws_neptune_network_security_restrictions` for better clarity. Consider incorporating checks for AWS Config rules and VPC flow logs as additional layers of security verification.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that all required network security configurations for Amazon Neptune clusters—such as VPC association, security group rules, and network ACLs—are fully accessible via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI). These properties are programmatically retrievable and auditable without manual console inspection. The audit steps described in the control can be mapped directly to API calls, making automation both feasible and reliable.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_neptune_cluster_network_security_restrictions

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: Amazon Neptune clusters, associated VPCs, security groups, and network ACLs
- API/CLI Method:
  - `boto3.client('neptune').describe_db_clusters()` or `aws neptune describe-db-clusters`
  - For each cluster, retrieve VPC and subnet IDs
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_security_groups(GroupIds=...)` or `aws ec2 describe-security-groups`
  - `boto3.client('ec2').describe_network_acls(Filters=...)` or `aws ec2 describe-network-acls`
- Properties to Check:
  - Neptune cluster is deployed within a VPC (not public)
  - Security groups attached to Neptune cluster subnets have restrictive inbound/outbound rules (e.g., only necessary ports open, limited source IP ranges)
  - Network ACLs for Neptune subnets do not allow broad or unrestricted access (e.g., no 0.0.0.0/0 for sensitive ports)
- Pass Criteria:
  - All Neptune clusters are in a VPC
  - Security groups restrict access to only required ports and trusted IPs
  - Network ACLs do not allow unrestricted inbound/outbound access
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any Neptune cluster is public or not in a VPC
  - Security groups allow wide-open access (e.g., 0.0.0.0/0 on Neptune ports)
  - Network ACLs allow unrestricted access

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation. The only minor difference was in the program name; I selected the more descriptive and specific name as suggested by the second opinion for clarity and alignment with best practices.

**SOURCE:** CIS AWS DATABASE SERVICES BENCHMARK V1
